title: "Deque"
category: "Linear Structures"
---

## Deque (Double-Ended Queue)

A deque (double-ended queue) allows insertion and removal at both the front and the back; implemented as a doubly-linked list, all four end operations are $O(1)$.

---

## Core Concept

Each node holds two pointers, `prev` and `next`, in addition to its data. The class keeps both a `head` and a `tail` pointer, so either end can be accessed and updated in constant time.

- Generalizes stack and queue: restrict it to one end and it degenerates into a stack or a queue.
- The doubly-linked list makes back-removal $O(1)$ too — a singly-linked list cannot.
- No fixed capacity; space grows with the number of elements.

---

## Operation Flow

1. `pushFront(v)`: the new node's `next` points to the old `head`, the old `head`'s `prev` points to the new node, then update `head`.
2. `pushBack(v)`: the symmetric operation on `tail`.
3. `popFront` / `popBack`: detach the end node and update the corresponding pointer; if the deque becomes empty, set both `head` and `tail` to null.

---

## Layout

<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 380 70" width="380"><g font-family="monospace" font-size="13"><text x="0" y="25">head                          tail</text><text x="0" y="50">null &lt;-&gt; 5 &lt;-&gt; 10 &lt;-&gt; 20 &lt;-&gt; null</text></g></svg>

> The visualizer shows a horizontal row of nodes with head and tail marked at the ends; four buttons map to the four end operations.

---

## Complexity Analysis

| Operation | Time | Space |
| --- | --- | --- |
| pushFront / pushBack | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| popFront / popBack | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| access by index | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Total Space | — | $O(n)$ |

$$T_{\text{push}} = T_{\text{pop}} = O(1)$$

All four end operations run in constant time, independent of the element count.

---

## Source Code

```cpp
struct Node {
    int val;
    Node* prev;
    Node* next;
    Node(int v) : val(v), prev(nullptr), next(nullptr) {}
};

void pushFront(int v) {
    Node* node = new Node(v);
    if (!head) { head = tail = node; }
    else {
        node->next = head;
        head->prev = node;
        head = node;
    }
    count++;
}
```

---

## Pros, Cons & When to Use

- Pro: $O(1)$ insertion and removal at both ends — more flexible than a singly-linked list.
- Pro: usable as a stack and as a queue at the same time.
- Con: an extra pointer per node raises memory overhead slightly; random access is still $O(n)$.
- Use for sliding-window problems, double-ended work queues, and algorithms needing both-end access (e.g. 0-1 BFS).

---

## Summary

- Doubly-linked list + head/tail pointers; all four end operations are $O(1)$.
- A common generalization of both the stack and the queue.
- Space $O(n)$, two pointers per node.
